seoTitle: Starrystone Tech Technical Support - Power Semiconductor Resources
seoDescription: Access Starrystone Tech technical documentation, application notes, selection guides, and FAE support. LiTong provides comprehensive technical resources for power management designs.

faqs.question: What technical resources does LiTong provide for Starrystone Tech products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive technical resources for Starrystone Tech products including datasheets, application notes, reference designs, selection guides, and evaluation boards. Our FAE team offers design review services, schematic and layout guidance, debugging support, and application-specific recommendations. We also provide sample kits for evaluation and can arrange factory training sessions for high-volume customers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our technical support team for access to documentation and design assistance. We can provide samples for evaluation.

faqs.question: How can I get samples of Starrystone Tech products?
faqs.answer: Samples of Starrystone Tech products are available through LiTong for qualified customers. To request samples, contact our sales team with your company information, project details, and required quantities. We typically provide small quantities (5-10 pieces) for evaluation purposes. For larger sample requirements or production ramp-up, we can discuss your forecast and schedule. Sample lead time is typically 1-2 weeks for standard products.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team with your project details to request samples. Provide as much application information as possible.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide schematic and layout review services?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong provides complimentary schematic and PCB layout review services for designs using Starrystone Tech products. Our FAE team reviews your design for proper component selection, circuit configuration, thermal management, and EMI considerations. We provide detailed feedback with recommendations for optimization. This service helps catch potential issues early, reducing debug time and improving design reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Submit your schematic and layout files to our FAE team for review. Early review is recommended before PCB fabrication.

faqs.question: What is the typical response time for technical inquiries?
faqs.answer: LiTong aims to respond to technical inquiries within 24 hours for standard questions. Complex design issues or questions requiring factory consultation may take 2-3 business days. For urgent production issues, we provide priority support with faster response times. Our FAE team is available during business hours (9 AM - 6 PM China time) for phone or video consultation.
faqs.decisionGuide: For urgent issues, contact us by phone. For detailed technical questions, email with supporting documentation.

faqs.question: Can LiTong help with design troubleshooting?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong provides troubleshooting support for designs using Starrystone Tech products. Our FAE team can help diagnose issues through schematic review, waveform analysis, and remote debugging. For complex issues, we can coordinate with Starrystone Tech factory engineers for deeper analysis. Common support areas include power conversion efficiency, thermal management, EMI issues, and protection circuit behavior.
faqs.decisionGuide: For troubleshooting, provide detailed problem description, schematic, and measurement data. Contact our FAE team for assistance.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide training on Starrystone Tech products?
faqs.answer: LiTong offers product training sessions for customers using Starrystone Tech products. Training topics include product overview, application design guidelines, troubleshooting techniques, and hands-on workshops. Training can be conducted at your facility, our offices, or via web conference. For high-volume customers, we can arrange customized training programs tailored to your specific applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us to schedule training sessions. We can customize content for your team's needs.

faqs.question: Where can I find the latest datasheets and application notes?
faqs.answer: The latest Starrystone Tech datasheets and application notes are available from LiTong. Contact our sales or technical support team to request documentation. We maintain the most current versions direct from the factory. Documentation packages typically include datasheets, application notes, reference design files, and software tools. We can also provide preliminary documentation for upcoming products under NDA.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our team to request documentation. Specify the products you're interested in.

faqs.question: What information should I provide when requesting technical support?
faqs.answer: When requesting technical support, please provide: detailed problem description, product part numbers, schematic and PCB layout files, bill of materials, measurement data or waveforms, operating conditions (voltage, temperature, load), and any error symptoms observed. The more information you provide, the faster we can diagnose and resolve your issue.
faqs.decisionGuide: Gather all relevant documentation before contacting support. Detailed information leads to faster resolution.

articles.title: AC-DC Controller Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting the right Starrystone Tech AC-DC controller based on application requirements, power level, and topology.

author.name: James Liu
author.title: Senior FAE - Power Solutions
author.bio: James has over 18 years of experience in AC-DC power supply design and has supported hundreds of successful customer projects.

content: Selecting the right AC-DC controller is critical for power supply performance, efficiency, and cost. This guide provides a systematic approach to choosing the optimal Starrystone Tech solution.
- **High Line Only (180-265VAC)**: Simplified design for fixed markets
- **Flyback**: Most common for isolated supplies under 150W
| Topology | Flyback QR | Flyback QR | Flyback QR |
- Consider synchronous rectification for high efficiency
- Target <75mW standby power for DoE Level VI
- Active PFC may be required for >75W
1. Core selection based on power level

Proper controller selection requires careful consideration of power requirements, input range, and application constraints. Starrystone Tech's product portfolio offers solutions for virtually any AC-DC application.
Contact LiTong's FAE team for personalized recommendations.

faeInsights.content: The most common mistake I see in AC-DC design is underestimating the importance of transformer design. The transformer affects efficiency, EMI, regulation, and reliability. I always recommend working with experienced transformer suppliers who understand flyback design. Another critical factor is the startup circuit - inadequate startup resistors can cause slow or failed startup, while oversized resistors waste standby power. For quasi-resonant controllers like the STR-A series, the valley detection is crucial for efficiency - ensure proper snubber design to get clean drain waveforms for reliable valley detection. Finally, don't overlook the feedback loop design - unstable loops can cause output ripple, poor transient response, or even oscillation.
keyTakeaways: Transformer design is critical - work with qualified suppliers
Startup circuit affects both startup time and standby power
Valley detection requires clean drain waveforms
Feedback loop stability must be verified across all conditions
EMI filtering should be designed early, not added later
commonPitfalls: Poor transformer design leading to efficiency loss or saturation
Inadequate startup circuit causing startup issues
Noisy drain waveforms affecting valley detection
Unstable feedback loop causing output issues
Insufficient EMI filtering causing certification failures
bestPractices: Work with experienced transformer suppliers for optimal designs
Calculate startup resistor for adequate startup with minimum loss
Design proper snubbers for clean switching waveforms
Verify feedback loop stability with load step testing
Implement EMI filtering from the beginning of design
faeInsights.insightLogic: The selection process should follow this decision tree: First, determine output power and input voltage range - this narrows the product selection significantly. Second, identify any special requirements like active PFC or synchronous rectification. Third, consider integration level - higher integration reduces BOM but may limit flexibility. Fourth, evaluate thermal requirements based on power density and enclosure constraints. Fifth, assess EMI requirements and available filter space. Finally, consider cost targets and production volume to optimize the solution.

customerCases.problem: The customer was designing their first USB charger and struggled with controller selection. They needed guidance on power level selection, transformer design, and meeting efficiency standards.
customerCases.diagnosis: After reviewing their requirements (5V/2A output, universal input, cost-sensitive), we determined STR-A6069H was the optimal choice with integrated MOSFET and quasi-resonant operation.
customerCases.solution: We provided the reference design and transformer specifications. Our FAE team reviewed their schematic and layout, providing optimization recommendations for efficiency and EMI.
results: Passed EMI testing on first attempt
Customer ordered 500K units in first year
customerCases.customerFeedback: LiTong's guidance was essential for our first power supply design. The controller selection and transformer recommendations were perfect for our application.
customerCases.challenge: The customer was designing their first USB charger and struggled with controller selection. They needed guidance on power level selection, transformer design, and meeting efficiency standards.
customerCases.feedback: Customer was satisfied with the technical support and product performance.

faqs.question: How do I choose between different STR-A series controllers?
faqs.answer: Select based on output power requirements: STR-A6069H for up to 30W, STR-A6080 for up to 45W, and STR-A6100 for up to 65W. All feature integrated 650V MOSFETs and quasi-resonant operation. The choice depends primarily on the integrated MOSFET current rating - 4A, 6A, or 8A respectively. Consider thermal constraints and efficiency requirements when selecting. Higher power controllers can be used for lower power applications if headroom is desired.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match controller to power level. Consider thermal and efficiency requirements.

faqs.question: What is quasi-resonant operation and why is it beneficial?
faqs.answer: Quasi-resonant (QR) operation switches the MOSFET at the voltage valley after transformer demagnetization. This reduces switching losses and EMI compared to hard switching. Benefits include higher efficiency, lower EMI, and reduced snubber requirements. The valley switching also reduces switching frequency at light loads, improving light-load efficiency. QR is particularly beneficial for universal input designs where switching losses are significant at high line voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: QR operation improves efficiency and reduces EMI automatically. No external configuration needed.

faqs.question: When do I need active PFC?
faqs.answer: Active Power Factor Correction (PFC) is typically required for power supplies above 75W to meet harmonic current regulations (IEC 61000-3-2). Some applications may require PFC at lower power levels depending on regional regulations or customer requirements. PFC improves power factor and reduces harmonic distortion. Starrystone Tech offers PFC controllers that can be combined with PWM controllers for high-power applications requiring PFC.
faqs.decisionGuide: PFC typically required above 75W. Check specific regulations for your target markets.

faqs.question: How important is synchronous rectification?
faqs.answer: Synchronous rectification (SR) replaces the output diode with a MOSFET, reducing rectifier losses. SR typically improves efficiency by 3-5%, which is significant for meeting efficiency standards and reducing thermal management requirements. SR is highly recommended for designs above 15W or where efficiency is critical. The STR-SR3010 synchronous rectifier controller works seamlessly with STR-A series PWM controllers for high-efficiency designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SR for designs above 15W or where efficiency is critical. 3-5% efficiency improvement.

faqs.question: What affects standby power consumption?
faqs.answer: Standby power is affected by: Controller quiescent current; Startup resistor value (higher is better but must ensure reliable startup); Feedback circuit power; and Synchronous rectifier controller consumption. Starrystone Tech controllers are designed for low standby power (<75mW). To minimize standby power: Use high-value startup resistors, optimize feedback circuit, and disable unnecessary functions in standby. Burst mode operation at light loads also helps reduce standby consumption.
faqs.decisionGuide: Optimize startup resistors and feedback circuit. Target <75mW for DoE Level VI compliance.

faqs.question: How do I meet EMI requirements?
faqs.answer: Meeting EMI requires attention to: Input filter design with common mode choke and X/Y capacitors; Proper PCB layout with minimized switching loops; Snubber circuits across switching devices; Transformer shielding if needed; and Grounding design. Start with a good EMI filter design from the reference design. Conduct pre-compliance testing early to identify issues. The quasi-resonant operation of STR-A series inherently produces lower EMI than fixed-frequency PWM.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design EMI filter from reference design. Test early. QR operation helps reduce EMI.

articles.title: LED Driver Selection and Application Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting Starrystone Tech LED drivers based on LED configuration, dimming requirements, and application needs.

author.name: Kevin Liu
author.title: FAE Manager - Lighting Solutions
author.bio: Kevin specializes in LED lighting solutions with extensive experience in driver design and thermal management.

content: Proper LED driver selection is essential for lighting performance, efficiency, and reliability. This guide covers key considerations for choosing Starrystone Tech LED drivers.
LEDs require constant current drive because:
- LED forward voltage varies with temperature and manufacturing
- Small voltage changes cause large current variations
- Proper current control maximizes LED lifetime
- **Buck-Boost**: Input can be above or below LED voltage
Successful LED driver selection requires understanding LED requirements, input power, and application constraints. Starrystone Tech offers drivers for various lighting applications.

faeInsights.content: The most critical aspect of LED driver design is proper thermal management. LEDs and drivers both generate heat, and excessive temperature reduces LED lifetime and driver reliability. I always recommend measuring actual temperatures in the final fixture under worst-case conditions. Another common issue is flicker during dimming - this is usually caused by PWM frequency that's too low or inadequate output capacitance. For PWM dimming, use frequencies above 200Hz to avoid visible flicker. When setting LED current, remember that higher current increases brightness but reduces LED lifetime and efficiency. It's often better to use more LEDs at lower current than fewer LEDs at higher current. Finally, don't forget about input voltage headroom - buck drivers need input voltage higher than LED string voltage plus headroom for proper regulation.
keyTakeaways: Thermal management is critical for LED lifetime and reliability
Use PWM frequency above 200Hz to prevent visible flicker
More LEDs at lower current often better than fewer at high current
Maintain adequate input voltage headroom for buck drivers
Test in actual fixture under worst-case thermal conditions
commonPitfalls: Inadequate thermal design reducing LED lifetime
PWM frequency too low causing visible flicker
Insufficient input voltage headroom affecting regulation
Inductor saturation causing instability
Poor PCB layout affecting performance and EMI
bestPractices: Measure temperatures in actual fixture under worst-case conditions
Use PWM dimming above 200Hz frequency
Optimize LED current for balance of brightness and lifetime
Ensure 2-3V headroom for buck driver operation
Use shielded inductors to minimize EMI
faeInsights.insightLogic: LED driver design follows this process: First, determine LED configuration (number, type, current) based on required light output and beam pattern. Second, calculate total LED voltage and ensure input power source can provide adequate voltage with headroom. Third, select driver topology based on input/output voltage relationship. Fourth, set current using proper sense resistor calculation. Fifth, select inductor with adequate saturation current. Sixth, design dimming circuit if required. Seventh, implement thermal management considering fixture constraints. Finally, validate through testing including efficiency, thermal, and dimming performance.

customerCases.problem: Customer needed help selecting LED driver for new downlight design with specific dimming and thermal requirements.
customerCases.diagnosis: Reviewed LED configuration and determined STR-L3201 was optimal with proper thermal design.
customerCases.solution: Provided complete driver design with thermal optimization and PWM dimming circuit.
customerCases.customerFeedback: The driver selection and thermal guidance were perfect for our application.
customerCases.challenge: Customer needed help selecting LED driver for new downlight design with specific dimming and thermal requirements.
customerCases.feedback: Customer was satisfied with the technical support and product performance.

faqs.question: How do I choose between buck and boost LED drivers?
faqs.answer: Choose buck (step-down) drivers when input voltage is higher than LED string voltage. Choose boost (step-up) drivers when LED string voltage is higher than input voltage. For battery-powered applications where input voltage varies above and below LED voltage, use buck-boost drivers. Starrystone Tech STR-L series are buck drivers for step-down applications. STR-H series includes boost drivers for high-voltage LED strings. Calculate LED string voltage (number of LEDs × forward voltage) and compare to input voltage range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use buck when Vin > Vled. Use boost when Vled > Vin. Calculate voltages carefully.

faqs.question: What is the optimal LED current for my application?
faqs.answer: Optimal LED current depends on brightness requirements, thermal constraints, and efficiency targets. Higher current increases brightness but reduces LED lifetime and efficacy (lumens per watt). Typical currents: 350mA for high-efficiency applications, 700mA for balanced performance, 1A or higher for maximum brightness. Consider using more LEDs at lower current rather than fewer LEDs at high current for better efficiency and lifetime. Always check LED datasheet for maximum current and thermal derating curves.
faqs.decisionGuide: Balance brightness, efficiency, and lifetime. More LEDs at lower current often optimal.

faqs.question: How do I prevent LED flicker?
faqs.answer: Prevent flicker by: Using PWM dimming frequency above 200Hz (invisible to human eye); Ensuring stable input voltage to driver; Using adequate output capacitance to smooth current; Implementing proper PCB layout to minimize noise; and Avoiding frequencies near 100-120Hz that can cause stroboscopic effects. For professional photography or broadcast applications, use frequencies above 1kHz. Test dimming performance in actual application environment as perceived flicker can vary with ambient conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PWM >200Hz. Test in actual environment. Consider 1kHz+ for professional applications.

faqs.question: What affects LED driver efficiency?
faqs.answer: LED driver efficiency is affected by: Switching losses (frequency and switching speed); Conduction losses (MOSFET RDS(on) and inductor DCR); Diode losses (use synchronous rectification for best efficiency); and Quiescent current. Buck drivers typically achieve 90-95% efficiency. Higher switching frequencies allow smaller components but may reduce efficiency. Higher LED currents generally improve efficiency (up to a point) due to reduced relative fixed losses. Proper component selection and PCB layout are critical for optimal efficiency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Expect 90-95% efficiency for buck drivers. Component selection and layout are critical.

faqs.question: Can I drive multiple LED strings with one driver?
faqs.answer: Single LED drivers are designed for one series string. For multiple strings, you have options: Use multiple drivers (one per string) for independent control; Use current balancing circuits (less common); or Connect strings in series if voltage allows (increases total voltage). Multiple drivers provide best current matching and reliability. Some Starrystone Tech drivers support multiple strings with internal current balancing - check specific product datasheets. Parallel connection of LED strings without current balancing is not recommended due to current mismatch.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use multiple drivers for best performance. Check for multi-string capable devices.

faqs.question: What protection features should I look for?
faqs.answer: Essential LED driver protections include: LED open protection (prevents over-voltage if string opens); LED short protection (limits current if LEDs short); Over-current protection (prevents excessive driver current); Over-temperature protection (reduces current or shuts down if overheating); and Under-voltage lockout (prevents operation with insufficient voltage). Starrystone Tech LED drivers include comprehensive protection. These features ensure safe operation and protect both the driver and LEDs from damage under fault conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Look for open/short protection, OTP, and OCP. Starrystone Tech drivers include comprehensive protection.

articles.title: Thermal Design Guide for Power Applications
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for thermal management in power supply and LED lighting applications using Starrystone Tech products.

author.name: Michael Chen
author.title: Senior FAE - Thermal Solutions
author.bio: Michael specializes in thermal management and reliability engineering with extensive experience in power electronics cooling.

content: Proper thermal design is critical for power supply reliability and lifetime. This guide covers thermal management techniques for Starrystone Tech applications.
- Switching losses: Function of frequency and voltage
Effective thermal design requires understanding heat sources, thermal paths, and cooling techniques. Proper thermal management ensures reliable, long-life operation.
Contact LiTong for thermal design support.

faeInsights.content: The most common thermal issue I see is inadequate copper area for heat spreading. Many designers underestimate how much copper is needed - for 1W of dissipation, you typically need 20-30mm² of copper area on a standard PCB. Another critical factor is thermal via design - vias need to be properly sized and plated to conduct heat effectively. I recommend using multiple small vias (0.3mm diameter) rather than a few large ones for better heat transfer. Component placement is also crucial - keep heat-generating components spread out to avoid hot spots, and position temperature-sensitive components (like electrolytic capacitors) away from heat sources. Finally, always test under worst-case conditions - maximum load, highest ambient temperature, and minimum airflow. Theoretical calculations are good, but actual measurement is essential.
keyTakeaways: Provide 20-30mm² copper area per watt of dissipation
Use multiple small thermal vias for effective heat transfer
Spread heat-generating components to avoid hot spots
Keep temperature-sensitive components away from heat sources
Always validate with thermal measurements under worst-case conditions
commonPitfalls: Insufficient copper area for heat spreading
Inadequate thermal vias limiting heat transfer
Concentrating heat sources creating hot spots
Placing capacitors near heat sources reducing lifetime
Relying only on calculations without measurement validation
bestPractices: Calculate copper area based on power dissipation requirements
Use arrays of small thermal vias (0.3mm) under hot components
Distribute heat-generating components across the PCB
Position electrolytic capacitors away from heat sources
Measure temperatures under worst-case operating conditions
faeInsights.insightLogic: Thermal design follows this process: First, calculate total power losses in the design including conduction, switching, and core losses. Second, identify the major heat sources and their power dissipation. Third, determine maximum allowable temperatures for each component based on datasheets and reliability requirements. Fourth, calculate required thermal resistance from junction to ambient. Fifth, design thermal management including copper area, vias, and heatsinks as needed. Sixth, position components to spread heat and protect sensitive parts. Seventh, validate through thermal measurement under worst-case conditions. Iterate if measured temperatures exceed limits.

customerCases.problem: Customer experienced thermal issues in compact adapter design with temperature exceeding limits at full load.
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis showed insufficient copper area and poor component placement concentrating heat.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned PCB with increased copper pours, optimized thermal vias, and better component placement.
results: Achieved continuous operation at full load
customerCases.customerFeedback: The thermal design guidance transformed our product. Proper copper area and via design made a huge difference.
customerCases.challenge: Customer experienced thermal issues in compact adapter design with temperature exceeding limits at full load.
customerCases.feedback: Customer was satisfied with the technical support and product performance.

faqs.question: How much copper area do I need for heat dissipation?
faqs.answer: As a general rule, provide 20-30mm² of copper area per watt of power dissipation for a standard 1.6mm FR4 PCB with 1oz copper. For example, a component dissipating 2W would need 40-60mm² of copper. This assumes the copper is connected to the component thermal pad or pins. Using thicker copper (2oz) or increasing PCB layer count improves heat spreading. For higher power densities, external heatsinks or forced air cooling may be needed. The actual requirement depends on ambient temperature, maximum allowed temperature, and airflow conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 20-30mm²/W as starting point. Increase for high ambient or limited airflow.

faqs.question: What is the best via configuration for thermal transfer?
faqs.answer: For thermal vias, use multiple small vias (0.3mm diameter) rather than a few large vias. A pattern of 9-16 vias (3x3 or 4x4 array) under a thermal pad provides excellent heat transfer to inner layers. Vias should be plated through with sufficient copper thickness. Fill vias with copper or thermal conductive material for best performance (though this adds cost). Keep vias close together (0.5-1mm spacing) under the hot component. The goal is to create a low thermal resistance path from the component to inner ground planes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 9-16 vias of 0.3mm diameter in array pattern. Place under thermal pad.

faqs.question: How do I calculate junction temperature?
faqs.answer: Calculate junction temperature using: Tj = Ta + (P × θja), where Tj is junction temperature, Ta is ambient temperature, P is power dissipation, and θja is thermal resistance from junction to ambient. For example: Tj = 40°C + (2W × 30°C/W) = 100°C. Thermal resistance θja depends on package type, PCB design, and airflow. SMD packages on standard PCBs typically have θja of 30-60°C/W. Packages with exposed thermal pads can achieve 15-30°C/W with proper PCB design. Always stay below the maximum rated junction temperature (typically 125-150°C).
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate Tj and ensure it's below maximum rating. Include safety margin.

faqs.question: What components are most sensitive to temperature?
faqs.answer: Electrolytic capacitors are the most temperature-sensitive components in power supplies. Their lifetime approximately halves for every 10°C temperature increase (Arrhenius equation). Keep electrolytic capacitors below 85-105°C depending on rating. Other sensitive components include: optocouplers (isolation degrades at high temperature), magnetic components (core losses increase), and semiconductors (performance changes, reduced lifetime). Position these components away from heat sources (MOSFETs, diodes, transformers) and ensure adequate cooling.
faqs.decisionGuide: Protect electrolytic capacitors from heat. Position sensitive components away from heat sources.

faqs.question: When do I need a heatsink?
faqs.answer: You need a heatsink when PCB-based cooling is insufficient to keep component temperatures within limits. Indicators: Calculated junction temperature exceeds rating; Component case temperature exceeds safe limits; or Required copper area exceeds available space. Heatsinks become necessary for power dissipation above 2-3W in compact designs. Options include: Aluminum heatsinks attached to components; Thermal interface materials improving contact; Forced air cooling for high power; or Heat pipes for very high power density. Consider cost, space, and reliability when deciding on heatsinking.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use heatsink when PCB cooling insufficient. Consider for >2-3W dissipation.

faqs.question: How do I measure temperature accurately?
faqs.answer: For accurate temperature measurement: Use fine-gauge thermocouples (36 AWG or smaller) attached with thermal conductive adhesive or tape; Place thermocouple junction in direct contact with measurement point; Allow thermal equilibrium (15-30 minutes) before recording; Shield thermocouple from air currents; and Calibrate measurement equipment. For IC junction temperature, measure case temperature and calculate: Tj = Tcase + (P × θjc). Infrared cameras provide good visualization of hot spots but may have accuracy limitations. Multiple measurement points provide complete thermal picture.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use proper thermocouple technique. Allow thermal equilibrium. Measure multiple points.

articles.title: EMI Design and Troubleshooting Guide
articles.summary: Practical guide for EMI reduction and EMC compliance in power supply designs using Starrystone Tech products.

author.name: Alex Wang
author.title: Senior FAE - EMC Solutions
author.bio: Alex specializes in electromagnetic compatibility with extensive experience in EMI troubleshooting and compliance testing.

content: EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) is a critical consideration for power supply designs. This guide covers EMI reduction techniques for Starrystone Tech applications.
- Common mode noise: Improve CM choke
Successful EMI management requires understanding sources, implementing proper filtering, and careful PCB layout. Early testing prevents costly redesigns.
Contact LiTong for EMI troubleshooting support.

faeInsights.content: The key to successful EMI design is starting early - don't wait until the end to test for EMI. I always recommend pre-compliance testing as soon as you have a working prototype. The most common EMI issue I see is inadequate input filtering, particularly the common mode choke. Many designers undersize the choke or use the wrong type. For conducted EMI, the common mode choke is your first line of defense - invest in a good quality choke with adequate inductance. Another critical factor is switching loop area - this is the loop formed by the switching transistor, transformer primary, and input capacitor. Keep this loop as small as possible to minimize magnetic field radiation. Use wide, short traces and place components close together. For transformer design, consider shielding windings to reduce capacitive coupling. Finally, don't forget about the secondary side - the output rectifier and its loop can also be a significant EMI source.
keyTakeaways: Start EMI testing early with pre-compliance scans
Invest in quality common mode choke for input filtering
Minimize switching loop areas in PCB layout
Consider transformer shielding for difficult designs
Secondary side rectifiers can also cause EMI issues
commonPitfalls: Waiting until final design to test for EMI
Inadequate common mode choke sizing
Large switching loops causing magnetic radiation
Missing snubbers for high-frequency ringing
Ignoring secondary side EMI contributions
bestPractices: Perform pre-compliance EMI testing on early prototypes
Size common mode choke based on conducted EMI requirements
Minimize primary and secondary switching loop areas
Add snubber circuits to damp high-frequency oscillations
Consider transformer shielding for Class B designs
faeInsights.insightLogic: EMI design follows this approach: First, understand the EMI sources in your topology - switching transistors, diodes, and transformers are the main culprits. Second, design proper input filtering with appropriately sized common mode choke and X/Y capacitors. Third, optimize PCB layout to minimize switching loop areas and provide good grounding. Fourth, implement snubber circuits if needed to damp high-frequency ringing. Fifth, consider transformer shielding for problematic designs. Sixth, perform pre-compliance testing early to identify issues. Seventh, troubleshoot any failures by identifying the frequency and nature of the problem, then apply targeted fixes. Iterate as needed until compliance is achieved.

customerCases.problem: Customer failed EMI testing with excessive conducted emissions at switching frequency harmonics.
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis showed inadequate common mode choke and large switching loop area on PCB.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned input filter with larger common mode choke and optimized PCB layout to minimize loops.
results: Passed Class B requirements with 6dB margin
No impact on efficiency or cost
customerCases.customerFeedback: The EMI troubleshooting guidance was excellent. Proper choke selection and layout made all the difference.
customerCases.challenge: Customer failed EMI testing with excessive conducted emissions at switching frequency harmonics.
customerCases.feedback: Customer was satisfied with the technical support and product performance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between common mode and differential mode EMI?
faqs.answer: Common mode EMI flows in the same direction on both line and neutral wires, returning through ground. It's caused by capacitive coupling to ground and common mode noise currents. Differential mode EMI flows between line and neutral (out on one, back on the other). It's caused by normal switching currents in the power supply. Common mode is typically addressed with common mode chokes and Y capacitors. Differential mode is addressed with X capacitors and differential inductors. Most power supplies have both types, requiring comprehensive filtering.
faqs.decisionGuide: Both types usually present. Use CM chokes and Y caps for CM, X caps for DM.

faqs.question: How do I select the right common mode choke?
faqs.answer: Select common mode choke based on: Inductance (typically 1-10mH for power supplies); Current rating (must handle maximum input current); DCR (lower is better for efficiency); and Frequency characteristics (effective at switching frequency). Higher inductance provides better filtering but increases size and cost. The choke should be rated for your maximum operating current with minimal saturation. Common mode impedance at switching frequency is the key parameter - check the manufacturer's impedance vs frequency curve.
faqs.decisionGuide: 1-10mH typical for power supplies. Ensure adequate current rating and check frequency characteristics.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of X and Y capacitors?
faqs.answer: X capacitors are connected between line and neutral (line to line) and filter differential mode noise. They're rated for AC line voltage and must be safety approved (X1, X2 rating). Y capacitors are connected between line/neutral and ground, filtering common mode noise. They have stricter safety requirements (Y1, Y2, Y4 rating) due to shock hazard if they fail short. Typical values: X capacitors 0.1-0.47μF, Y capacitors 1000-4700pF. Total Y capacitance is limited by leakage current requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: X caps for differential mode, Y caps for common mode. Use safety-rated capacitors.

faqs.question: How do snubber circuits reduce EMI?
faqs.answer: Snubber circuits (typically RC networks) damp high-frequency ringing caused by parasitic inductance and capacitance. This ringing occurs during switching transitions and can generate EMI at frequencies much higher than the switching frequency. The snubber provides a lossy path that dissipates the ringing energy, reducing the amplitude of oscillations. Common locations for snubbers are across the primary MOSFET (drain-source) and across the output rectifier. Component values are typically determined empirically - start with 100-1000pF capacitor and 10-100Ω resistor.
faqs.decisionGuide: Add snubbers to damp ringing across switches and rectifiers. Tune values empirically.

faqs.question: What PCB layout practices reduce EMI?
faqs.answer: Key PCB layout practices for EMI reduction: Minimize switching loop areas (primary and secondary); Use ground planes to reduce noise coupling; Keep high-speed switching traces short and wide; Separate noisy power circuits from sensitive control circuits; Place input filter components close to input connector; and Use stitching vias to connect ground planes. The primary switching loop (input cap, MOSFET, transformer primary) is most critical - keep this loop as small as possible.
faqs.decisionGuide: Minimize switching loops, use ground planes, separate noisy and sensitive circuits.

faqs.question: When should I use shielding for EMI control?
faqs.answer: EMI shielding is typically needed when: PCB-based techniques are insufficient; The design must meet very stringent limits (Class B with margin); Physical size constraints prevent adequate filtering; or Radiated emissions are problematic. Shielding options include: Transformer shielding windings (reduces capacitive coupling); Copper foil shields on PCBs; and Metal enclosures or shields. Shielding adds cost and complexity, so try filtering and layout optimization first. Transformer shielding is often the most effective for isolated power supplies.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use shielding when filtering/layout insufficient. Try transformer shielding first.
